facts_disclosed: Shore house in Wildwood with brother Kevin

turns.content: What's the best way to get a deck re-stained. Do it myself or hire somebody.

Depends on the size of the deck and how much time you want to put in. If it's a straightforward job -- pressure wash, let it dry, apply stain -- doing it yourself can save you $500-$1,000 or more. Hiring someone makes sense if the deck has a lot of damage, tricky railings, or you just don't have the time. How big is the deck?
It's not huge. Maybe 12 by 16. It's on our shore house down in Wildwood. Me and my brother Kevin own the place together. Went in on it about 15 years ago.

A 12x16 deck is very doable as a DIY project. For a shore house that size, you're looking at maybe a weekend of work -- one day to pressure wash and prep, let it dry, then a second day to stain. The salt air in Wildwood is tough on wood though, so you'll want a solid exterior stain with UV protection. Something like Cabot or TWP. Are you thinking solid stain or semi-transparent?
Semi-transparent. Nothing fancy. The place isn't a palace. It's a regular shore house. But it's ours and I want to keep it up.

Smart approach. Semi-transparent will let the wood grain show through while still protecting it from the sun and salt. Here's the basic process:

1. Pressure wash the deck -- use around 1,500-2,000 PSI so you clean it without gouging the wood.
2. Let it dry completely, at least 48 hours.
3. Sand any rough spots or splinters.
4. Apply the stain with a roller or pad applicator for the flat boards, brush for railings and edges.
5. One coat is usually enough for semi-transparent, but a second coat on high-traffic areas doesn't hurt.

For Wildwood, I'd do this in late spring before the summer season hits.
Yeah that's the plan. Get down there Memorial Day weekend with Kevin and knock it out before the summer renters show up. We rent it a few weeks in July to cover the taxes.

That's a solid plan. Memorial Day weekend gives you good weather and enough time before July renters arrive. If you and your brother split the work, you could easily knock it out in a day and a half. Cost-wise you're looking at maybe $100-$150 for stain, plus pressure washer rental if you don't own one -- usually around $75-$100 for a day. Way cheaper than hiring it out.
Kevin's got a pressure washer. He's the one with all the tools. I'm the one who does the work.

Ha, sounds like a good arrangement -- one supplies the gear, the other supplies the labor. That brings your cost down to just the stain and maybe some brushes. Pick up the stain ahead of time so you're not wasting half the weekend at a hardware store in Wildwood.
Good call. I'll grab it up here in Philly before we head down. Anything else I should bring.

A few things worth having on hand:

- Painter's tape for any areas you want to protect (siding, door frames)
- Drop cloths if the deck is adjacent to anything you don't want stain splatter on
- A stir stick -- stain separates in the can
- A paint tray for the roller
- Sandpaper or a sanding block for rough spots (80-100 grit)
- Old clothes you don't care about

That should cover it. Straightforward job.
What about the boards themselves. A couple are starting to warp. Do I replace those before I stain or just leave them.

If they're noticeably warped or have soft spots, replace them before you stain. No point in putting a nice finish on wood that's going to give out. You can pick up pressure-treated deck boards at any lumber yard -- just make sure the new boards are the same thickness as the existing ones. If they're only slightly cupped, you can sometimes flip them over and screw them back down. The salt air down there accelerates wood rot, so it's worth checking every board while you're at it.
Affirmative. I'll check them all when I get down there and pull anything that's shot. Rather do it right once than come back and fix it later.

That's the way to do it. Check for any loose screws or nails while you're at it -- a full inspection before you stain means you won't have to touch it again for another two or three years. The shore air is hard on everything, but if you stay ahead of it the deck will last.
15 years and still standing. Not bad for two guys who didn't know what they were doing when they bought the place.
Sounds like you and your brother figured it out along the way. 15 years of keeping a shore house maintained is no small thing. Enjoy Wildwood this summer.
